title: Recommendation: How to summarize recovery work after the setup incident
summary: Use a recovery update that leads with symptoms and a single safe next action, then clearly segments guidance by OS (macOS/Linux verified; Windows still validating) and avoids claiming universal resolution until Windows is confirmed.

claims.text: Start the recovery message with symptoms (especially partial-install confusion) and then give one safe next action to reduce uncertainty about rerunning setup, credential clearing, or waiting for a patched CLI.

Avoid broad language like “resolved” unless the environment caveat is prominently included, because engineering verification is currently limited to macOS and Linux and Windows recovery steps are still being validated.

Structure the customer-facing update into three explicit sections—who is affected, what action to take, and which environments are still pending validation—and keep the Windows caveat in the first paragraph rather than as a footnote.

When recommending actions, point users to the patched CLI’s recovery command (available in the release candidate) because it addresses stale credentials and cleans partial-install state; align documentation/screenshots before broad promotion.

Match support’s OS-segmented guidance (verified macOS, verified Linux, pending Windows) and ensure communications do not direct users to the old installer flow once the release candidate is promoted (Windows screenshots are still pending).

openQuestions: What exact wording will be used on the status page to reflect macOS/Linux verified recovery while Windows validation (including the credential-cache issue) is still in progress?
When will Windows recovery validation complete, and when will Windows screenshots/docs be ready so comms can safely promote the release candidate path?
What is the single “safe command” string that should be consistently used across status updates, support macros, and customer-success messages?
recommendation: Publish a recovery update that (1) opens with the partial-install symptoms and one safe recovery command, (2) clearly segments instructions by OS with Windows marked as “validation in progress” in the first paragraph, (3) avoids “fully resolved” claims until Windows is verified, and (4) coordinates timing with docs so customers are not pointed to the old installer flow once the release candidate is promoted.
